policy.rule: Replace LaTeX only when context, definitions, and structural constraints make the reconstruction high confidence. Preserve the paper-as-printed form for suspected paper errors, and leave ambiguous formulas unchanged with an explicit note.

evidence: The author TeX contains the three displayed lines consecutively at MarkovianOrderBook.tex:660-662.
Direct algebraic expansion of the first line gives 1+4p_1p_n-2p_1-2p_n, not the second printed line.
The surrounding Markov-chain argument does not state enough here to justify silently replacing the final covariance expression.
alternatives: Treat the first line as E[X_1X_n] rather than covariance.
Under an additional zero-mean/stationary initialization, rederive covariance from the transition matrix before replacing the printed result.
ref_cont_de_larrard_markovian_lob_1104.4.note: Semantic review found an internal algebraic inconsistency in the paper's three-line covariance derivation. Confidence is insufficient to choose a corrected covariance without an additional initialization assumption, so the machine-decoded LaTeX is intentionally left unchanged.

evidence: The balanced-order-book proof defines t_n=n log(n)t and uses zeta(n)=n log(n).
Substituting zeta(n)=n log(n) into zeta(n)/log(zeta(n)) is asymptotic to n.
The immediately preceding Donsker scaling uses Z(t pi lambda n / D(f))/sqrt(n), which requires N_{t_n} to be order n.
assumptions: The balanced-case time scale zeta(n)=n log(n) established earlier in the proof applies.
ref_cont_de_larrard_markovian_lob_1104.4.note: Reconstructed the full asymptotic chain from the proof context. The paper-as-printed final term drops a factor n; the retained correction restores the order-n event count required by the following Donsker limit.

ref_tran_bin_2003.00598:formula:0005.note: Reconstructed from the BiN temporal-normalization definition and checked dimensionally: averaging T vectors in R^D yields the mean vector used in equations (5b)-(5d).

evidence: This is the componentwise temporal-slice standard deviation in BiN equation (5b).
The Hadamard square and mean over T preserve dimension D.

evidence: This standardizes every temporal column using the D-dimensional mean and standard deviation from (5a)-(5b).

ref_tran_bin_2003.00598:formula:0007.note: Reconstructed as elementwise centering and scaling along the temporal mode. Broadcast shapes on both sides are D by T.

evidence: This is the feature-slice sample mean defined by BiN equation (6a).

The author subequation is at manuscript.tex:124.
ref_tran_bin_2003.00598:formula:0009.note: Reconstructed from the feature-mode definition: averaging D feature slices in R^T yields the T-dimensional mean used by equations (6b)-(6d).

evidence: This is the componentwise feature-slice standard deviation in BiN equation (6b).
The result is a T-vector, matching the mean in (6a) and required broadcast in (6c).
The author subequation is at manuscript.tex:125.
ref_tran_bin_2003.00598:formula:0010.note: Reconstructed as the elementwise standard deviation across D feature slices; the resulting T-vector is required by the next normalization step.

evidence: Equation (6b) immediately defines sigma_1 as the feature-mode T-dimensional standard deviation.
Using sigma_1 gives 1_D sigma_1^T shape D by T, matching X^(i).
The printed sigma_2 is D-dimensional, so 1_D sigma_2^T has shape D by D and cannot normalize a general D by T input.
The surrounding prose explicitly says equations (6a)-(6d) normalize along the feature dimension.
assumptions: D and T are not accidentally constrained to the same value.
ref_tran_bin_2003.00598:formula:0011.note: Corrected a high-confidence paper typo: the denominator must use the feature-mode standard deviation sigma_1, not temporal-mode sigma_2. Only sigma_1 produces the D by T broadcast required by the equation.

evidence: This is the learnable componentwise scale-and-shift step in BiN equation (6d).
